items.title: Federal Independent Dispute Resolution Operations
items.reason: This is a Federal Register final rule directly implementing No Surprises Act provisions on the IDR process, disclosure requirements, claim coding standards, and administrative procedures—core regulatory developments that revenue-cycle teams must track and implement.
items.summary: The HHS/DOL/Treasury agencies issued final rules in the Federal Register implementing key No Surprises Act provisions governing the Federal Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) process established under the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021. The rules require group health plans and health insurance issuers to disclose specified information with initial payment or denial notices for surprise-billing-protected services, and to use claim adjustment reason codes (CARCs) and remittance advice remark codes (RARCs) when communicating electronically or in paper form with out-of-network entities. The final rules also amend requirements for the open negotiation period before IDR initiation, IDR eligibility reviews, administrative and certified IDR entity fee collection, bundled payment definitions, batched service handling, and timeframe extensions for extenuating circumstances. Revenue-cycle teams must update payment processing, remittance protocols, and dispute-handling procedures to comply with these finalized IDR operational requirements.

items.title: Lower No Surprises Act dispute fees officially go into effect - Radiology Business
items.reason: This item reports on a concrete regulatory change to the Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) fee structure under the No Surprises Act, which directly affects revenue-cycle operations and dispute resolution costs.
items.summary: Lower dispute fees for the No Surprises Act's Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) process have officially taken effect. The reduction in IDR filing and administration fees represents a regulatory implementation change that directly impacts healthcare provider costs when resolving out-of-network billing disputes. This fee reduction affects all providers and payers involved in the IDR process and is relevant to revenue-cycle teams managing dispute resolution expenses and cash flow related to surprise billing cases.

items.title: Trump administration reforms surprise billing dispute resolution - Healthcare Dive
items.reason: The item reports on Trump administration reforms to the Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) process under the No Surprises Act, which directly addresses federal rulemaking and implementation of balance-billing protections.
items.summary: The Trump administration has announced reforms to the surprise billing dispute resolution process established under the No Surprises Act. The reforms modify how the Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) process operates for resolving out-of-network billing disputes between insurers and healthcare providers. These changes affect healthcare providers, insurers, and patients by altering the procedural framework for resolving balance-billing disputes and determining payment determinations outside normal adjudication. Revenue-cycle teams must monitor these procedural changes as they directly impact dispute timelines, documentation requirements, and payment outcomes for claims subject to IDR.

items.title: AHA provides recommendations to CMS on proposed rule for interoperability standards and prior authorization for drugs - American Hospital Association
items.reason: The item directly addresses CMS proposed rules on prior authorization and interoperability standards, which are core components of federal prior-authorization reform that directly impact revenue-cycle operations.
items.summary: The American Hospital Association submitted recommendations to CMS regarding a proposed rule that establishes interoperability standards and prior-authorization requirements for drugs. The AHA's input addresses how CMS's upcoming prior-authorization rule will affect hospitals' administrative processes and drug coverage determinations. This proposed rule is part of CMS's broader interoperability mandate and will establish new standards that hospitals and healthcare systems must implement for prior-authorization workflows. Revenue-cycle teams will need to monitor this rule's final requirements, timelines, and technical standards to ensure compliance with new payer coordination and authorization submission processes once the rule is finalized.

items.title: OIG: 3 Largest MA Insurers Deny Prior Auth Requests at High Rates for Long-Term Acute Care, Inpatient Rehab - MedCity News
items.reason: This item directly addresses prior authorization denial practices by major MA payers, which is a key enforcement and policy issue within prior authorization reform discussions affecting healthcare revenue cycle operations.
items.summary: The Office of Inspector General (OIG) has identified that the three largest Medicare Advantage insurers are denying prior authorization requests at elevated rates for long-term acute care and inpatient rehabilitation services. This OIG finding documents specific payer behavior patterns in prior authorization denials, which is relevant to revenue-cycle teams managing claim approvals and patient access for these service lines. High denial rates for prior authorization requests directly impact claim processing timelines, revenue capture, and provider-payer relationships, making this enforcement finding significant for organizations tracking payer compliance and prior authorization performance metrics.

items.title: House Committee Moves to Block Medicare AI Prior Authorization Pilot - Telehealth.org
items.reason: This addresses a federal CMS prior-authorization policy development (AI pilot program), which directly falls within the scope of CMS interoperability/prior-auth rule changes that affect healthcare revenue-cycle operations.
items.summary: A House Committee has moved to block a Medicare AI prior-authorization pilot program. This action represents Congressional intervention in CMS's use of artificial intelligence for prior-authorization decision-making, which would impact how Medicare processes authorization requests. The development affects revenue-cycle teams by potentially preventing or delaying implementation of an AI-driven prior-auth system that could have changed authorization timelines and workflows for providers and payers processing Medicare claims.

items.title: West Virginia Changes Prior Authorization Law After Man's Treatment Delay and Death - AJMC
items.reason: This item directly addresses a state-level prior-authorization policy change, which falls squarely within the topic definition of state changes to payer prior-authorization requirements.
items.summary: West Virginia enacted legislation reforming its prior authorization requirements following a case in which treatment delays contributed to a patient's death. The state law change modifies how payers must process prior-authorization requests, though specific requirements and effective dates are not detailed in this excerpt. This state-level policy shift affects how healthcare providers and payers in West Virginia manage authorization workflows and directly impacts revenue-cycle operations by potentially reducing approval delays and associated administrative burden.

items.title: Ohio House Passes Prior Authorization Reform to Reduce Delays and Improve Patient Care - Ohio House of Representatives (.gov)
items.reason: This is a state-level prior authorization reform measure directly addressing payer authorization requirements and delays, which falls within the scope of prior authorization policy changes affecting revenue-cycle operations.
items.summary: The Ohio House of Representatives passed prior authorization reform legislation aimed at reducing authorization delays and improving patient care. The bill represents a state-level policy shift to streamline payer prior-authorization requirements, which directly impacts revenue-cycle teams by potentially reducing claim submission delays, appeal processes, and administrative burden related to authorization denials. Revenue-cycle teams in Ohio and multi-state payers will need to monitor implementation details, effective dates, and any updates to payer policies that may result from this legislation, as changes to state authorization timelines and requirements affect claim processing workflows and cash flow timing.

items.title: Latest prior auth survey shows promised reform remains elusive - American Medical Association
items.reason: The AMA survey directly addresses the status of prior authorization reform implementation, which is central to the topic of federal and state prior-auth policy changes and their real-world effectiveness.
items.summary: The American Medical Association released a survey indicating that prior authorization reform initiatives have not yet achieved their intended results in reducing administrative burden on healthcare providers. While the excerpt does not provide specific reform rules, effective dates, or quantitative findings, the survey appears to assess the gap between promised prior-auth improvements and actual implementation across the healthcare system. This matters for revenue-cycle teams because it suggests that despite regulatory efforts to streamline prior authorization, operational challenges may persist in the approval process, affecting claims processing timelines and denials management strategies.

items.title: The missing piece in prior authorization reform? Providers, some argue. - Healthcare Brew
items.reason: The article directly addresses prior authorization reform and the role of providers in that reform process, which is central to the topic definition of federal and state changes to payer prior-authorization requirements.
items.summary: Healthcare Brew reports on arguments from providers that they represent a missing component in prior authorization reform efforts. The article discusses provider perspectives on how PA reform has been shaped primarily around payer and regulatory initiatives, suggesting that provider input and operational considerations have been underrepresented in policy discussions. This is relevant to revenue-cycle teams because provider engagement in PA reform directly affects how authorizations are requested, processed, and paid, influencing claims processing workflows and reimbursement timelines. The piece highlights a structural gap in reform advocacy that could impact how new PA requirements are implemented in practice.
